docs.title: Aniwa
docs.text:
Universal dataset profiling and intelligence.

Aniwa helps developers, analysts, data engineers, and modern data teams understand datasets quickly through intelligent profiling, quality analysis, statistical summaries, and beautiful reports.

docs.title: Quick Installation
docs.text: pip install aniwa

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text:
aniwa customers.csv

Generate HTML report:

aniwa customers.csv --report html

docs.title: Aniwa Philosophy
docs.text:
See your data clearly.

Aniwa is more than a dataset profiler.

It is designed to become a modern, developer-first platform for understanding, validating, and trusting data across environments, workflows, and ecosystems.

The philosophy behind Aniwa guides: - architecture decisions - user experience - contributor workflows - feature design - long-term product direction

Every feature added to Aniwa should align with these principles.

docs.title: Universal
docs.text:
Aniwa is designed to work with data everywhere.

Modern data exists across: - files - databases - warehouses - pipelines - notebooks - APIs - cloud systems

Aniwa aims to provide a consistent profiling experience regardless of where the data comes from.

docs.title: What This Means
docs.text:
• support for multiple file formats
  • support for databases and warehouses
  • cross-platform usability
  • minimal ecosystem lock-in
  • interoperability with modern data workflows

docs.title: Guiding Belief
docs.text: Users should not need to change their workflow to understand their data.

docs.title: Developer-First
docs.text:
Aniwa is built primarily for developers, analysts, engineers, researchers, and technical teams.

Developer experience is treated as a core feature, not an afterthought.

docs.title: What This Means
docs.text:
• intuitive CLI design
  • excellent defaults
  • minimal setup
  • readable outputs
  • clear documentation
  • automation-friendly workflows

docs.title: Guiding Belief
docs.text: Data tooling should feel elegant, productive, and enjoyable to use.

docs.title: Fast
docs.text:
Profiling should be efficient and responsive.

Users should be able to understand datasets quickly without unnecessary complexity or overhead.

Aniwa prioritizes performance from the beginning.

docs.title: What This Means
docs.text:
• efficient dataframe operations
  • scalable profiling logic
  • lightweight execution paths
  • fast and deep profiling modes
  • optimized report generation

docs.title: Guiding Belief
docs.text: Profiling should accelerate workflows, not slow them down.

docs.title: Modular
docs.text:
Aniwa is designed with clear boundaries between components.

The system should remain easy to: - extend - test - maintain - contribute to

docs.title: What This Means
docs.text:
• isolated report systems
  • independent readers
  • reusable profiling components
  • clear project structure
  • future plugin support

docs.title: Guiding Belief
docs.text: Good architecture enables long-term innovation.

docs.title: Intelligent
docs.text:
Aniwa should not only display statistics.

It should help users understand the meaning, quality, and reliability of their data.

docs.title: What This Means
docs.text:
• profiling insights
  • quality detection
  • semantic inference
  • anomaly detection
  • trust-oriented analysis

Future versions of Aniwa aim to provide: - contextual understanding - AI-assisted profiling - semantic column detection - intelligent recommendations

docs.title: Guiding Belief
docs.text: Understanding data requires more than counting rows and columns.

docs.title: Automation-Friendly
docs.text:
Aniwa is designed to integrate naturally into modern engineering workflows.

Profiling should work: - locally - in CI/CD pipelines - in scripts - in scheduled jobs - in orchestration systems

docs.title: What This Means
docs.text:
• CLI-first workflows
  • machine-readable outputs
  • structured reports
  • deterministic behavior
  • future pipeline integrations

docs.title: Guiding Belief
docs.text: Profiling should become part of the development lifecycle.

docs.title: Long-Term Vision
docs.text:
Aniwa aims to evolve into a universal platform for:

  • dataset profiling
  • data intelligence
  • trust analysis
  • quality monitoring
  • semantic understanding
  • automated validation

The long-term vision is to help individuals and organizations:

understand data faster, trust data more confidently, and build better systems around data.

docs.title: Design Philosophy
docs.text:
Aniwa favors:

Principle Preference Simplicity over unnecessary complexity Clarity over excessive abstraction Performance over heavy frameworks Extensibility over rigid systems Developer Experience over enterprise friction Practical Intelligence over gimmicks

docs.title: Open Source Philosophy
docs.text:
Aniwa is built as an open-source project because data tooling benefits from: - transparency - collaboration - extensibility - shared innovation

Contributions from the community are encouraged and welcomed.

docs.title: Final Thought
docs.text:
Aniwa is built on a simple idea:

Data understanding should be universal, intelligent, and accessible.

Every release should move closer to that goal.

docs.title: Adding File Readers
docs.text:
This guide explains how to add new dataset readers to Aniwa.

Aniwa is designed to be:

universal and extensible

which means adding support for new dataset formats should be:

  • predictable
  • modular
  • scalable
  • contributor-friendly

docs.title: Purpose of File Readers
docs.text:
File readers are responsible for:

  • detecting supported dataset formats
  • loading datasets
  • converting datasets into Polars DataFrames

The reader layer acts as:

Aniwa’s ingestion system

docs.title: Current Reader Architecture
docs.text:
Current reader systems are located in:

aniwa/io/

docs.title: Current Reader Structure
docs.text:
Typical structure:

aniwa/io/
├── readers.py

Future versions may evolve into:

aniwa/io/
├── readers/
│   ├── csv_reader.py
│   ├── json_reader.py
│   ├── parquet_reader.py
│   ├── excel_reader.py
│   └── registry.py

docs.title: Current Supported Formats
docs.text:
Aniwa currently supports:

Format Extension CSV .csv JSON .json Excel .xlsx, .xls Parquet .parquet

docs.title: Reader Philosophy
docs.text:
Readers should remain:

simple, isolated, and efficient

docs.title: Why Isolation Matters
docs.text:
Reader isolation improves:

  • maintainability
  • debugging
  • scalability
  • contributor onboarding

docs.title: Core Reader Responsibilities
docs.text:
Every reader should:

  • validate format support
  • load data safely
  • return a Polars DataFrame
  • handle errors gracefully

docs.title: Standard Reader Flow
docs.text:
Typical execution flow:

file path
→ reader selection
→ parser
→ Polars DataFrame

docs.title: Reader Output Requirement
docs.text:
All readers must return:

polars.DataFrame

docs.title: Why Polars Is Required
docs.text:
Using a single dataframe engine ensures:

  • consistency
  • performance
  • simplified profiling logic

docs.title: Current Reader Entry Point
docs.text:
Current systems typically route through:

read_dataset()

docs.title: Example Reader Dispatch
docs.text:
Typical logic:

if suffix == ".csv":
    return pl.read_csv(path)

docs.title: Supported Reader Patterns
docs.text:
Readers may support:

Pattern direct parsing wrapper parsing conversion pipelines

docs.title: Reader Design Principles
docs.text:
Readers should prioritize:

Principle Purpose correctness accurate loading simplicity maintainability performance fast execution predictability stable behavior

docs.title: Step 1 — Choose the Format
docs.text:
Identify the new dataset format.

Examples:

Format XML Avro ORC Feather SQLite

docs.title: Step 2 — Evaluate Parsing Libraries
docs.text:
Choose a reliable parser.

Examples:

Format Library XML lxml Avro fastavro ORC pyarrow SQLite sqlite3

docs.title: Step 3 — Create Reader Logic
docs.text: Add logic inside the reader system.

docs.title: Example Reader Structure
docs.text:
Example:

if suffix == ".xml":
    return read_xml(path)

docs.title: Step 4 — Convert to Polars
docs.text:
All parsed datasets should become:

pl.DataFrame

docs.title: Example Conversion
docs.text:
Example:

pl.DataFrame(records)

docs.title: Step 5 — Add Extension Support
docs.text:
Register supported extensions.

Example:

SUPPORTED_EXTENSIONS = [
    ".csv",
    ".json",
    ".xml",
]

docs.title: Step 6 — Add Error Handling
docs.text:
Readers should provide:

  • helpful errors
  • graceful failures
  • clear diagnostics

docs.title: Good Error Example
docs.text:
Example:

raise DatasetReadError(
    "Unsupported dataset type '.xml'."
)

docs.title: Step 7 — Add Tests
docs.text: Every new reader must include tests.

docs.title: Required Reader Tests
docs.text:
Tests should validate:

Test successful loading invalid files malformed datasets empty datasets edge cases

docs.title: Step 8 — Add Example Datasets
docs.text:
Place example files in:

examples/

docs.title: Why Example Files Matter
docs.text:
Examples improve:

  • contributor onboarding
  • debugging
  • testing

docs.title: Step 9 — Update Documentation
docs.text:
Update:

  • README.md
  • supported formats docs
  • usage examples

docs.title: Step 10 — Verify CLI Integration
docs.text:
Test end-to-end execution:

aniwa dataset.xml

docs.title: Reader Architecture Guidelines
docs.text:

docs.title: Why Stateless Readers Matter
docs.text:

docs.title: Performance Philosophy
docs.text:

docs.title: Why Reader Performance Matters
docs.text:

docs.text:

docs.title: Security Philosophy
docs.text:

  • controlled execution
  • predictable behavior

docs.title: Unsafe Reader Patterns to Avoid
docs.text:
Avoid:

  • arbitrary code execution
  • unsafe deserialization
  • untrusted evaluation

docs.title: Error Handling Philosophy
docs.text:
Readers should fail:

clearly and gracefully

docs.title: Logging Philosophy
docs.text:
Future readers may support:

docs.title: Reader Scalability Philosophy
docs.text:
Readers should be designed for:

docs.title: Reader Anti-Patterns
docs.text:
Avoid:

Anti-Pattern duplicated parsing logic row-by-row loops hardcoded assumptions silent failures

docs.title: Contributor Best Practices
docs.text:
Contributors should prioritize:

  • readability
  • maintainability
  • performance
  • testing

docs.title: Example Reader Template
docs.text:
Basic example:

def read_xml(path: str) -> pl.DataFrame:
    records = parse_xml(path)
    return pl.DataFrame(records)

    assert df.height > 0

docs.title: Adding Insights
docs.text:
This guide explains how to create, extend, and maintain Aniwa’s insight generation system.

Insights are one of the most important parts of Aniwa because they transform:

raw statistics

into:

human-readable intelligence

docs.title: Purpose of Insights
docs.text:
Insights help users quickly identify:

  • suspicious patterns
  • quality problems
  • unusual structures
  • possible risks
  • meaningful observations

without manually inspecting every metric.

docs.title: Why Insights Matter
docs.text:
Most users do not want to manually analyze:

  • null percentages
  • uniqueness counts
  • cardinality distributions
  • statistics tables

Insights simplify interpretation.

docs.title: Insight Philosophy
docs.text:
Aniwa’s insight system is designed to be:

Principle Purpose modular isolated insight rules explainable understandable logic scalable future expansion lightweight fast execution useful actionable output

docs.title: Insight Architecture Overview
docs.text:
High-level flow:

DataFrame
→ profiling metrics
→ insight rules
→ Insight objects
→ reports

docs.title: Insight System Location
docs.text:
Current insight systems typically live in:

aniwa/core/

or:

aniwa/insights/

depending on future architecture evolution.

docs.title: Current Insight Flow
docs.text:
Conceptually:

profile_dataframe()
↓
generate metrics
↓
evaluate rules
↓
create insights
↓
attach to DatasetProfile

docs.title: What an Insight Contains
docs.text:
Each insight typically contains:

Field Purpose level severity message human-readable explanation

docs.title: Example Insight
docs.text:
Example:

Insight(
    level="warning",
    message="Column 'email' may contain sensitive information."
)

docs.title: Insight Severity Levels
docs.text:
Current levels may include:

Level info warning critical

docs.title: Severity Philosophy
docs.text:
Severity levels should communicate:

importance and urgency

docs.title: INFO Insights
docs.text:
INFO insights describe:

  • informational observations
  • low-risk findings
  • helpful context

docs.title: WARNING Insights
docs.text:
WARNING insights describe:

  • potential issues
  • suspicious patterns
  • data quality concerns

docs.title: CRITICAL Insights
docs.text:
CRITICAL insights describe:

  • severe quality issues
  • dangerous inconsistencies
  • major dataset problems

docs.title: Current Insight Categories
docs.text:
Aniwa currently supports insights such as:

Insight duplicate rows sparse columns possible PII high-cardinality columns

docs.title: Insight Design Principles
docs.text:
Good insights should be:

Principle Meaning actionable users know what it means concise short and clear relevant avoids noise explainable predictable logic

docs.title: Bad Insight Example
docs.text:
Avoid vague insights like:

Something looks suspicious.

docs.title: Good Insight Example
docs.text:
Prefer:

Column 'customer_id' contains 100% unique values and may represent an identifier.

docs.title: Insight Generation Strategy
docs.text: Insights are typically rule-based.

docs.title: Why Rule-Based Systems Matter
docs.text:
Rule-based systems are:

  • explainable
  • deterministic
  • testable
  • maintainable

docs.title: Example Insight Rule
docs.text:
Example:

if duplicate_percent > 10:
    add_warning(...)

docs.title: Step 1 — Identify a Useful Pattern
docs.text:
Choose a dataset characteristic worth highlighting.

Examples:

docs.title: Code Style Guide
docs.text:
This document defines the official coding standards, architectural conventions, formatting principles, and engineering practices used throughout Aniwa.

The goal of this guide is to ensure that Aniwa remains:

  • maintainable
  • scalable
  • readable
  • contributor-friendly
  • production-quality

over the long term.

docs.title: Philosophy
docs.text:
Aniwa code should feel:

simple, clean, modern, scalable, and intentional

docs.title: Why Code Style Matters
docs.text:
Consistent code style improves:

  • maintainability
  • onboarding
  • debugging
  • scalability
  • collaboration

docs.title: Core Engineering Principles
docs.text:
Aniwa follows several engineering principles:

Principle Meaning readability code should be easy to understand simplicity avoid unnecessary complexity modularity isolated responsibilities scalability future-friendly design consistency predictable patterns maintainability sustainable engineering

docs.title: General Philosophy
docs.text:
Prefer code that is:

clear over clever

docs.title: Readability First
docs.text:
Readable code is more valuable than:

  • compressed code
  • overly clever abstractions
  • unnecessary optimizations

docs.title: File Organization Philosophy
docs.text:
Files should remain:

focused and modular

docs.title: Good File Characteristics
docs.text:
A good file should:

  • have a clear responsibility
  • remain reasonably sized
  • avoid unrelated logic

docs.title: Bad File Characteristics
docs.text:
Avoid files that:

  • contain many unrelated systems
  • mix responsibilities
  • become excessively large

docs.title: Module Philosophy
docs.text:
Modules should represent:

single conceptual responsibilities

docs.title: Example Good Separation
docs.text:
Example:

readers.py
statistics.py
insights.py

docs.title: Naming Philosophy
docs.text:
Names should be:

  • descriptive
  • predictable
  • explicit

docs.title: Good Variable Names
docs.text:
Examples:

dataset_profile
duplicate_rows
null_percentage

docs.title: Bad Variable Names
docs.text:
Avoid:

x
data2
tmp

unless context is extremely obvious.

docs.title: Function Naming
docs.text:
Functions should describe:

what they do

docs.title: Good Function Names
docs.text:
Examples:

read_dataset()
render_html_report()
generate_insights()

docs.title: Bad Function Names
docs.text:
Avoid vague names like:

handle()
process()
run()

without context.

docs.title: Class Naming
docs.text:
Classes should use:

PascalCase

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
Examples:

DatasetProfile
ProfileMetadata

docs.title: Function Length Philosophy
docs.text:
Functions should remain:

small and focused

docs.title: Why Small Functions Matter
docs.text:
Small functions improve:

  • readability
  • testing
  • debugging

docs.title: Recommended Function Characteristics
docs.text:
Functions should ideally:

  • perform one task
  • remain easy to scan
  • avoid excessive nesting

docs.title: Avoid Deep Nesting
docs.text:
Prefer:

early returns

over:

deeply nested conditionals

docs.title: Example Preferred Style
docs.text:
Good:

if not data:
    return

docs.title: Example Avoided Style
docs.text:
Avoid:

if data:
    if something:
        if another:

docs.title: Line Length
docs.text:
Recommended line length:

88–100 characters

docs.title: Why Reasonable Line Length Matters
docs.text:
This improves:

  • readability
  • diffs
  • side-by-side review

docs.title: Import Organization
docs.text: Imports should be grouped logically.

docs.title: Import Order
docs.text:
Recommended order:

# standard library

# third-party libraries

# local imports

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
Example:

import json
from pathlib import Path

import polars as pl
import typer

from aniwa.core.profiler import profile_dataframe

docs.title: Type Hint Philosophy
docs.text:
Aniwa strongly encourages:

type hints

docs.title: Why Type Hints Matter
docs.text:
Type hints improve:

  • readability
  • tooling
  • maintainability
  • debugging

docs.title: Example Type Hints
docs.text:
Example:

def load_config(
    file_path: str,
) -> dict[str, Any]:

docs.title: Avoid Ambiguous Types
docs.text:
Prefer:

dict[str, Any]

over:

dict

docs.title: Enum Philosophy
docs.text:
Use enums for:

  • controlled values
  • CLI options
  • internal constants

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
Example:

class ReportFormat(str, Enum):

docs.title: Constants Philosophy
docs.text: Shared constants should be centralized.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
Example:

CONFIG_FILE_NAMES = (...)

docs.title: Avoid Magic Values
docs.text: Avoid unexplained hardcoded values.

docs.title: Bad Example
docs.text:
Avoid:

if x > 37:

docs.title: Good Example
docs.text:
Prefer:

MAX_NULL_THRESHOLD = 37

docs.title: Comment Philosophy
docs.text:
Comments should explain:

why

not:

what

docs.title: Avoid Obvious Comments
docs.text:
Avoid:

# increment count
count += 1

docs.title: Good Comments
docs.text:
Prefer comments that explain:

  • reasoning
  • architecture
  • tradeoffs

docs.title: Docstring Philosophy
docs.text: Public functions should include docstrings.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
Example:

"""
Profile a dataset.
"""

docs.title: Error Handling Philosophy
docs.text:
Errors should be:

  • explicit
  • informative
  • actionable

docs.title: Good Error Example
docs.text:
Example:

raise typer.BadParameter(
    "Invalid profiling mode."
)

docs.title: Avoid Silent Failures
docs.text: Never suppress important failures silently.

docs.title: Exception Philosophy
docs.text:
Catch exceptions only when:

  • adding context
  • recovering gracefully
  • improving UX

docs.title: Avoid Broad Exceptions
docs.text:
Avoid:

except:

docs.title: Prefer Explicit Exceptions
docs.text:
Prefer:

except ValueError:

docs.title: Logging Philosophy
docs.text:
Future logging systems should prioritize:

  • clarity
  • usefulness
  • debuggability

docs.title: Architecture Philosophy
docs.text:
Systems should remain:

modular and layered

docs.title: Separation of Concerns
docs.text:
Avoid mixing:

Concern Example profiling reporting rendering parsing CLI business logic

docs.title: Business Logic Placement
docs.text:
Business logic belongs in:

core systems

not:

CLI or templates

docs.title: Template Philosophy
docs.text:
Templates should contain:

  • presentation logic only

docs.title: Reader Philosophy
docs.text:
Readers should:

  • only ingest data
  • avoid profiling logic

docs.title: Renderer Philosophy
docs.text:
Renderers should:

  • only render outputs
  • avoid business logic

docs.title: Testing Philosophy
docs.text: Every important feature should include tests.

docs.title: Test Organization
docs.text:
Tests belong in:

tests/

docs.title: Recommended Test Naming
docs.text:
Examples:

test_readers.py
test_reports.py
test_insights.py

docs.title: Test Philosophy
docs.text:
Tests should validate:

  • correctness
  • edge cases
  • regressions

docs.title: Edge Cases Matter
docs.text:
Always test:

  • empty datasets
  • malformed inputs
  • invalid configs
  • extreme values

docs.title: Performance Philosophy
docs.text: Optimize only when necessary.

docs.title: Avoid Premature Optimization
docs.text:
Prefer:

clarity first

before:

micro-optimizations

docs.title: Vectorization Philosophy
docs.text:
Prefer:

Polars vectorized operations

over:

row-by-row loops

docs.title: Why Vectorization Matters
docs.text:
Vectorization improves:

  • speed
  • scalability
  • memory efficiency

docs.title: Configuration Philosophy
docs.text:
Configuration systems should remain:

  • predictable
  • validated
  • user-friendly

docs.title: CLI Philosophy
docs.text:
CLI systems should prioritize:

  • simplicity
  • discoverability
  • consistency

docs.title: Naming Conventions
docs.text: Element Convention files snake_case functions snake_case classes PascalCase constants UPPER_CASE variables snake_case

docs.title: File Naming Examples
docs.text:
Good:

json_report.py
config_loader.py

Bad:

JsonReport.py
configLoader.py

docs.title: Formatting Tools
docs.text:
Recommended tools:

Tool Purpose black formatting ruff linting pytest testing

docs.title: Black Formatting
docs.text:
Recommended command:

black .

docs.title: Ruff Linting
docs.text:
Recommended command:

ruff check .

docs.title: Pytest
docs.text:
Recommended command:

pytest

docs.title: Pull Request Philosophy
docs.text:
PRs should remain:

  • focused
  • reviewable
  • isolated

docs.title: Good Pull Requests
docs.text:
Good PRs:

  • solve one problem
  • include tests
  • update documentation

docs.title: Avoid Large Mixed PRs
docs.text:
Avoid PRs that:

  • solve unrelated issues
  • refactor everything simultaneously
  • mix formatting with features

docs.title: Documentation Philosophy
docs.text:
Documentation is considered:

core infrastructure

docs.title: Why Documentation Matters
docs.text:
Good documentation improves:

  • contributor scalability
  • project longevity
  • onboarding

docs.title: Contributor Philosophy
docs.text:
Contributors should prioritize:

  • clarity
  • maintainability
  • architectural consistency

docs.title: Long-Term Engineering Philosophy
docs.text:
Aniwa is designed for:

long-term sustainability

not:

short-term hacks

docs.title: Future Scalability Philosophy
docs.text:
Code written today should remain understandable:

years later

docs.title: Final Philosophy
docs.text:
Aniwa code should feel:

intentional, elegant, scalable, and deeply maintainable

Every contributor helps shape:

the future architecture of the project

docs.text:

docs.title: Dependency Management
docs.text:
This document explains how Aniwa manages dependencies, packages, tooling, versioning, compatibility, and long-term ecosystem stability.

Dependency management is a critical part of maintaining a:

stable, scalable, and production-quality open-source project

docs.title: Purpose of Dependency Management
docs.text:
Dependency management ensures that:

  • installations remain reproducible
  • environments remain stable
  • contributors can collaborate reliably
  • releases remain predictable
  • compatibility issues are minimized

docs.title: Dependency Philosophy
docs.text:
Aniwa follows several dependency principles:

Principle Purpose minimalism avoid unnecessary packages stability reduce breaking changes maintainability manageable ecosystem performance lightweight installs reproducibility predictable environments

docs.title: Core Philosophy
docs.text:
Aniwa should depend only on packages that are:

necessary, reliable, and actively maintained

docs.title: Why Dependency Discipline Matters
docs.text:
Poor dependency management causes:

  • installation failures
  • version conflicts
  • unstable releases
  • security risks
  • contributor frustration

docs.title: Current Dependency System
docs.text:
Aniwa currently uses:

pyproject.toml

as the primary dependency management system.

docs.title: Why pyproject.toml Matters
docs.text:
pyproject.toml provides:

  • modern packaging standards
  • dependency management
  • build configuration
  • project metadata

docs.title: Current Build System
docs.text:
Aniwa currently uses:

setuptools
wheel

docs.title: Example Build Configuration
docs.text:
Example:

[build-system]
requires = ["setuptools>=68", "wheel"]
build-backend = "setuptools.build_meta"

docs.title: Primary Dependency File
docs.text:
Main dependency definitions live in:

pyproject.toml

docs.title: Why Centralized Dependencies Matter
docs.text:
Centralization improves:

  • consistency
  • reproducibility
  • maintenance

docs.title: Current Core Dependencies
docs.text:
Aniwa currently depends on libraries such as:

Dependency Purpose polars dataframe engine typer CLI framework rich terminal UI pydantic structured models jinja2 template rendering openpyxl Excel support PyYAML YAML configs

docs.title: Why These Libraries Were Chosen
docs.text:
Libraries were selected based on:

  • maturity
  • performance
  • ecosystem quality
  • maintainability

docs.title: Polars Philosophy
docs.text:
Aniwa uses:

Polars

instead of pandas because it provides:

  • higher performance
  • better scalability
  • modern dataframe architecture

docs.title: Typer Philosophy
docs.text:
Aniwa uses:

Typer

because it provides:

  • clean CLI development
  • type-hint integration
  • excellent UX

docs.title: Rich Philosophy
docs.text:
Aniwa uses:

Rich

to provide:

  • beautiful terminal interfaces
  • readable output
  • modern CLI experiences

docs.title: Pydantic Philosophy
docs.text:
Aniwa uses:

Pydantic

for:

  • structured validation
  • serialization
  • typed models

docs.title: Dependency Categories
docs.text: Aniwa dependencies generally fall into categories.

docs.title: Runtime Dependencies
docs.text:
Required during execution.

Examples:

Dependency polars typer rich

docs.title: Development Dependencies
docs.text:
Used for development workflows.

Examples:

Dependency pytest black ruff

docs.title: Optional Dependencies
docs.text:
Future systems may support:

optional dependency groups

docs.title: Why Optional Dependencies Matter
docs.text:
Optional dependencies reduce:

  • installation weight
  • unnecessary complexity

docs.title: Future Dependency Groups
docs.text:
Potential groups:

Group dev docs database cloud AI

docs.title: Example Future Extras
docs.text:
Example:

pip install aniwa[database]

docs.title: Version Pinning Philosophy
docs.text:
Aniwa should avoid:

overly strict pinning

unless necessary.

docs.title: Why Over-Pinning Is Dangerous
docs.text:
Strict pinning can cause:

  • dependency conflicts
  • ecosystem incompatibility
  • installation issues

docs.title: Recommended Version Strategy
docs.text:
Prefer:

package>=minimum_version

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
Example:

polars>=1.0.0

docs.title: Compatibility Philosophy
docs.text:
Aniwa should maintain compatibility with:

  • stable Python releases
  • actively maintained libraries

docs.title: Current Python Support
docs.text:
Aniwa currently targets:

Python 3.10+

docs.title: Why Modern Python Matters
docs.text:
Modern Python versions provide:

  • performance improvements
  • better typing
  • cleaner language features

docs.title: Avoid Unmaintained Libraries
docs.text:
Dependencies should be:

  • actively maintained
  • stable
  • well-documented

docs.title: Dependency Evaluation Checklist
docs.text:
Before adding a dependency, evaluate:

Question Is it actively maintained? Is it widely trusted? Does it solve a real problem? Is the dependency necessary? Does it increase complexity?

docs.title: Avoid Dependency Bloat
docs.text:
Avoid adding packages for:

  • tiny helper functions
  • unnecessary abstractions
  • trivial utilities

docs.title: Why Minimalism Matters
docs.text:
Fewer dependencies improve:

  • reliability
  • install speed
  • maintainability

docs.title: Security Philosophy
docs.text:
Dependencies introduce:

supply chain risk

docs.title: Security Best Practices
docs.text:
Dependencies should be:

  • audited
  • updated responsibly
  • monitored for vulnerabilities

docs.title: Future Security Tooling
docs.text:
Future systems may include:

Tool pip-audit Dependabot security CI checks

docs.title: Updating Dependencies
docs.text:
Dependencies should be updated:

carefully and incrementally

docs.title: Why Incremental Updates Matter
docs.text:
Large dependency jumps can introduce:

  • breaking changes
  • hidden bugs
  • ecosystem instability

docs.title: Dependency Update Workflow
docs.text:
Recommended workflow:

update
→ test
→ validate
→ release

docs.title: Testing After Dependency Changes
docs.text:
Always run:

pytest

after dependency updates.

docs.title: Additional Recommended Checks
docs.text:
Recommended commands:

ruff check .
black .

docs.title: Virtual Environment Philosophy
docs.text:
Always use:

isolated virtual environments

docs.title: Why Virtual Environments Matter
docs.text:
Virtual environments prevent:

  • package conflicts
  • global pollution
  • inconsistent behavior

docs.title: Creating Virtual Environments
docs.text:
Example:

python -m venv .venv

docs.title: Activating Environments
docs.text:

docs.title: Testing Guide
docs.text:
This document explains the testing philosophy, architecture, workflows, strategies, and long-term validation systems used in Aniwa.

Testing is one of the most important engineering systems in the project because it ensures:

  • reliability
  • stability
  • scalability
  • contributor confidence
  • release quality

Aniwa treats testing as:

core infrastructure

not an optional development step.

docs.title: Purpose of Testing
docs.text:
Testing ensures that:

  • features work correctly
  • regressions are prevented
  • releases remain stable
  • contributors can collaborate safely

docs.title: Testing Philosophy
docs.text:
Aniwa testing prioritizes:

Principle Purpose correctness features behave properly reproducibility predictable outcomes maintainability sustainable engineering scalability long-term project growth confidence safer contributions

docs.title: Core Philosophy
docs.text:
Aniwa aims for:

high-confidence engineering

through:

predictable automated validation

docs.title: Why Testing Matters
docs.text:
Without testing:

  • regressions increase
  • contributors break existing systems
  • releases become unstable
  • debugging becomes expensive

docs.title: Current Testing Framework
docs.text:
Aniwa currently uses:

pytest

docs.title: Why pytest Was Chosen
docs.text:
pytest provides:

  • clean syntax
  • strong ecosystem
  • scalability
  • excellent developer experience

docs.title: Test Directory Structure
docs.text:
Tests are stored in:

tests/

docs.title: Example Test Structure
docs.text:
Example:

tests/
├── test_cli.py
├── test_readers.py
├── test_reports.py
├── test_config.py
├── test_insights.py

docs.title: Test Organization Philosophy
docs.text:
Tests should mirror:

the architecture of the project

docs.title: Why Mirrored Structures Matter
docs.text:
Mirrored structures improve:

  • discoverability
  • maintainability
  • contributor onboarding

docs.title: Core Testing Categories
docs.text: Aniwa currently focuses on several testing layers.

docs.title: Unit Tests
docs.text:
Unit tests validate:

individual functions and modules

docs.title: Integration Tests
docs.text:
Integration tests validate:

systems working together

docs.title: CLI Tests
docs.text:
CLI tests validate:

  • commands
  • options
  • argument handling
  • UX behavior

docs.title: Reader Tests
docs.text:
Reader tests validate:

  • dataset loading
  • supported formats
  • malformed files

docs.title: Report Tests
docs.text:
Report tests validate:

  • rendering
  • export generation
  • output structure

docs.title: Config Tests
docs.text:
Config tests validate:

  • YAML loading
  • TOML loading
  • JSON loading
  • override priority

docs.title: Insight Tests
docs.text:
Insight tests validate:

  • rule triggering
  • severity correctness
  • false positives

docs.title: Metadata Tests
docs.text:
Metadata tests validate:

  • runtime tracking
  • report metadata
  • command reconstruction

docs.title: Current Testing Flow
docs.text:
Typical development flow:

implement
→ test
→ validate
→ refactor
→ release

docs.title: Running Tests
docs.text:
Run all tests using:

pytest

docs.title: Running Verbose Tests
docs.text:
Example:

pytest -v

docs.title: Running Specific Tests
docs.text:
Example:

pytest tests/test_cli.py

docs.title: Running Individual Test Functions
docs.text:
Example:

pytest tests/test_cli.py::test_help

docs.title: Why Granular Testing Matters
docs.text:
Granular testing improves:

  • debugging speed
  • iteration speed
  • contributor productivity

docs.title: Testing Philosophy
docs.text:
Tests should be:

Principle Meaning isolated independent deterministic repeatable readable understandable maintainable sustainable

docs.title: Deterministic Testing
docs.text:
Tests should always produce:

predictable outcomes

docs.title: Why Determinism Matters
docs.text:
Non-deterministic tests create:

  • flaky CI
  • contributor confusion
  • unreliable validation

docs.title: Good Test Naming
docs.text: Good test names explain behavior.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
Examples:

test_read_csv()
test_invalid_config()
test_render_html_report()

docs.title: Bad Test Names
docs.text:
Avoid vague names like:

test_stuff()
test_feature()

docs.title: Test Function Philosophy
docs.text:
Each test should validate:

one clear behavior

docs.title: Why Small Tests Matter
docs.text:
Small tests improve:

  • readability
  • debugging
  • maintainability

docs.title: Assertion Philosophy
docs.text:
Assertions should remain:

explicit and readable

docs.title: Good Assertion Example
docs.text:
Example:

assert df.height == 5

docs.title: Avoid Weak Assertions
docs.text:
Avoid:

assert result

when more specific validation is possible.

docs.title: Edge Case Philosophy
docs.text:
Always test:

  • invalid inputs
  • empty datasets
  • malformed files
  • extreme values

docs.title: Why Edge Cases Matter
docs.text:
Most real-world failures happen at:

system boundaries

docs.title: Example Edge Cases
docs.text:
Examples:

Case empty CSV corrupted JSON unsupported extension invalid config values

docs.title: Reader Testing Philosophy
docs.text:
Readers should validate:

  • correct parsing
  • format handling
  • conversion to Polars

docs.title: Example Reader Test

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
Aniwa supports reusable configuration files for simplifying profiling workflows.

Configuration files allow teams and developers to define default profiling behavior without repeatedly passing CLI arguments.

This improves:

  • reproducibility
  • developer experience
  • automation
  • CI/CD workflows
  • consistency across teams

docs.title: Supported Configuration Formats
docs.text:
Aniwa currently supports:

  • YAML
  • TOML
  • JSON

Supported filenames:

aniwa.yaml
aniwa.yml
aniwa.toml
aniwa.json

docs.title: Automatic Configuration Discovery
docs.text:
Aniwa automatically searches the current working directory for configuration files.

Example:

aniwa examples/customers.csv

If a supported config file exists, Aniwa automatically loads it.

docs.title: Configuration Priority
docs.text:
Aniwa resolves configuration using the following priority order:

CLI arguments > Config file > Defaults

This means command-line arguments always override configuration values.

docs.title: YAML Example
docs.text:
aniwa.yaml

mode: deep

report:
  format: html
  template: dark
  output_dir: reports/

sections:
  include:
    - summary
    - statistics
    - insights

docs.title: TOML Example
docs.text:
aniwa.toml

mode = "deep"

[report]
format = "html"
template = "dark"
output_dir = "reports/"

[sections]
include = ["summary", "statistics", "insights"]

docs.title: JSON Example
docs.text:
aniwa.json

{
  "mode": "deep",
  "report": {
    "format": "html",
    "template": "dark",
    "output_dir": "reports/"
  },
  "sections": {
    "include": [
      "summary",
      "statistics",
      "insights"
    ]
  }
}

docs.title: Using Custom Config Files
docs.text:
You can specify a custom config file using:

aniwa examples/customers.csv \
  --config examples/config_sample.yaml

This is useful for:

  • multiple environments
  • reusable presets
  • testing workflows
  • CI pipelines

docs.text:
mode: deep

Supported values:

  • fast
  • deep

  format: html

  output_dir: reports/

Aniwa automatically:

  • creates directories

  • applies extensions

docs.text:
sections:
  include:
    - summary
    - statistics

docs.title: Excluded Sections
docs.text:

  exclude:
    - charts

docs.text:
Config:

mode: deep

CLI:

aniwa dataset.csv --mode fast

Result:

fast mode is used

docs.title: Override Template
docs.text:
Config:

  template: dark

CLI:

aniwa dataset.csv --template enterprise

Result:

enterprise template is used

docs.title: Internal Configuration Flow
docs.text:
Aniwa internally performs:

  1. config discovery
  2. config parsing
  3. validation
  4. flattening
  5. merging with CLI arguments
  6. final runtime resolution

docs.title: Configuration Architecture
docs.text:
Configuration handling is primarily implemented in:

aniwa/config.py
aniwa/config_loader.py
aniwa/cli.py

Responsibilities:

docs.title: config_loader.py
docs.text:
Responsible for:

  • reading YAML
  • reading TOML
  • reading JSON
  • parsing config structures
  • flattening nested config values

docs.title: cli.py
docs.text:
Responsible for:

  • discovering config files
  • loading configs
  • merging CLI overrides
  • validating runtime configuration

docs.title: Config Validation
docs.text:
Aniwa validates:

  • report format
  • profiling mode
  • report sections
  • template names

Invalid configs raise helpful errors.

Example:

Invalid mode in config: ultra

docs.title: Missing Config Files
docs.text:
Missing config files do not crash Aniwa.

Example:

Warning: Configuration file not found.
Using defaults.

docs.text:
Recommended project setup:

project/
├── datasets/
├── reports/
├── aniwa.yaml

Example:

report:
  format: html
  template: enterprise
  output_dir: reports/

Then simply run:

aniwa datasets/customers.csv

docs.text:
Example GitHub Actions workflow:

- name: Run Aniwa
  run: |
    aniwa datasets/customers.csv

Since config is auto-discovered, workflows remain clean and reproducible.

docs.title: Basic Profiling
docs.text:
Run:

aniwa examples/customers.csv

Aniwa automatically:

  1. validates the dataset path
  2. detects dataset type
  3. loads the dataset
  4. profiles schema information
  5. analyzes quality
  6. calculates statistics
  7. generates insights
  8. renders a report

docs.title: What Aniwa Detects
docs.text:
Aniwa analyzes:

  • dataset dimensions
  • column names
  • data types
  • missing values
  • duplicate rows
  • uniqueness
  • numeric distributions
  • suspicious patterns
  • potential IDs
  • potential sensitive information

pip install aniwa

docs.text:

  • enterprise
  • dark

aniwa examples/customers.csv \
  --report html \
  --template dark

docs.text:
Automatically generate reports into directories.

aniwa examples/customers.csv \

  --output-dir reports/

Aniwa automatically:

  • generates filenames
  • applies correct extensions

reports/aniwa_report.html

docs.text:
Generate focused reports.

aniwa examples/customers.csv \

docs.title: Exclude Sections
docs.text:
Example:

aniwa examples/customers.csv \
  --exclude statistics

docs.title: Supported Sections
docs.text:

  • summary
  • schema
  • quality
  • statistics
  • insights
  • charts

docs.title: Configuration Files
docs.text:
Aniwa supports reusable project configurations.

Supported config files:

  • aniwa.yaml
  • aniwa.yml
  • aniwa.toml
  • aniwa.json

Example:

mode: deep

report:
  format: html
  template: dark
  output_dir: reports/

sections:
  include:
    - summary
    - statistics
    - insights

Run:

aniwa examples/customers.csv

Aniwa automatically discovers and loads the config.

docs.title: Custom Config File
docs.text:
Use a specific config file:

aniwa examples/customers.csv \
  --config examples/config_sample.yaml

docs.title: CLI Overrides Config
docs.text:
CLI arguments always override config values.

Example:

aniwa examples/customers.csv \
  --mode fast

Overrides:

mode: deep

docs.title: Why Aniwa Exists

Data professionals constantly work with:

Before trusting a dataset, teams need answers to questions like:

  • Are there duplicates?

Aniwa exists to make answering those questions:

docs.text:
Aniwa currently supports:

Chart Purpose Null Percentage Chart visualize missing values Unique Values Chart visualize cardinality Duplicate Overview Chart visualize duplicate ratios

docs.title: Supported Report Formats
docs.text:
Charts currently appear in:

  • HTML reports
  • PDF reports

Charts are not currently supported in:

  • console reports
  • JSON reports
  • markdown reports

docs.title: Chart Architecture
docs.text:
Aniwa charts are generated using:

  • Chart.js
  • Jinja2 templates
  • embedded JSON chart data

docs.title: Rendering Pipeline
docs.text:
Chart rendering flow:

profile_dataframe()
→ dataset profile
→ chart data serialization
→ Jinja template
→ Chart.js rendering
→ final HTML/PDF

docs.title: Chart Data Serialization
docs.text:
Aniwa injects serialized chart data into templates.

Example:

<script id="aniwa-chart-data" type="application/json">
{
    "columns": [...],
    "nullPercents": [...],
    "uniqueCounts": [...]
}
</script>

This allows frontend chart rendering without additional APIs.

docs.title: Null Percentage Chart
docs.text:
The null percentage chart visualizes missing values across columns.

Purpose:

  • identify sparse columns
  • detect ingestion issues
  • locate incomplete fields

docs.title: Example Use Cases
docs.text:
Useful for:

  • ETL debugging
  • governance validation
  • ML preprocessing
  • schema validation

docs.title: Interpretation
docs.text:
High null percentages may indicate:

  • missing source data
  • optional fields
  • ingestion failures
  • schema mismatches

docs.title: Unique Values Chart
docs.text:
The unique values chart visualizes column cardinality.

Purpose:

  • identify ID-like fields
  • detect categorical imbalance
  • understand uniqueness patterns

docs.title: Interpretation
docs.text:
High cardinality may indicate:

  • IDs
  • UUIDs
  • emails
  • hashes
  • transaction references

Low cardinality may indicate:

  • categories
  • enums
  • flags
  • labels

docs.title: Duplicate Overview Chart
docs.text:
The duplicate chart visualizes:

  • duplicate rows
  • unique rows

Purpose:

  • identify duplication severity
  • evaluate dataset quality
  • support governance checks

docs.title: Doughnut Chart Usage
docs.text:
Aniwa currently uses doughnut charts for duplicate visualization.

Reasoning:

  • compact layout
  • intuitive proportions
  • visually clear comparison

docs.title: Chart.js Integration
docs.text:
Aniwa uses Chart.js because it provides:

  • lightweight rendering
  • responsive charts
  • strong browser compatibility
  • simple configuration
  • modern visuals

docs.title: Why Frontend Rendering?
docs.text:
Aniwa renders charts client-side instead of pre-generating images.

Benefits:

  • smaller reports
  • interactive rendering
  • responsive layouts
  • reduced backend complexity

docs.title: Chart Responsiveness
docs.text:
Charts automatically adapt to:

  • desktop screens
  • tablets
  • mobile devices

This is achieved using responsive Chart.js options.

docs.title: Example Responsive Config
docs.text:
options: {
    responsive: true
}

docs.title: Chart Layout System
docs.text:
Aniwa uses CSS grid layouts for charts.

Example:

.charts {
    display: grid;
    grid-template-columns: repeat(2, 1fr);
}

This enables:

  • balanced layouts
  • responsive rendering
  • scalable dashboard structures

docs.title: Full-Width Charts
docs.text:
Some charts span multiple columns.

Example:

.chart-card.full {
    grid-column: 1 / -1;
}

Useful for:

  • overview charts
  • large visualizations
  • executive summaries

docs.title: Chart Styling Philosophy
docs.text:
Aniwa charts prioritize:

  • readability
  • simplicity
  • clarity
  • performance
  • accessibility

Charts should never overwhelm the report.

docs.title: Template-Specific Styling
docs.text:
Different templates apply different chart aesthetics.

Examples:

Template Style clean minimal compact dense enterprise polished dark dark-mode optimized

docs.title: Dark Mode Charts
docs.text:
Dark templates customize:

  • grid colors
  • axis colors
  • backgrounds
  • datasets

Example:

const darkAxis = "#cbd5e1";

docs.title: Chart Colors
docs.text:
Current charts use semantic color systems.

Examples:

Meaning Color nulls blue duplicates red uniqueness teal warnings yellow

docs.title: Accessibility Considerations
docs.text:
Aniwa charts aim to support:

  • high contrast
  • readable labels
  • color clarity
  • scalable sizing

Future goals include:

  • colorblind-safe palettes
  • screen reader support
  • WCAG compliance

docs.title: Chart Performance
docs.text:
Large datasets can impact rendering performance.

Potential bottlenecks:

  • thousands of columns
  • massive cardinality
  • wide datasets

docs.title: Future Optimization Strategies
docs.text:
Potential future optimizations:

  • lazy rendering
  • chart pagination
  • sampling
  • virtualization
  • WebGL rendering

docs.title: Future Planned Charts
docs.text:
Planned future visualizations include:

  • correlation matrices
  • histograms
  • outlier plots
  • drift visualizations
  • distribution plots
  • heatmaps
  • embeddings
  • semantic clusters

docs.title: Statistical Visualization Roadmap
docs.text:
Future numeric charts may include:

  • box plots
  • violin plots
  • percentile distributions
  • skewness visualization

docs.title: Governance Visualization Roadmap
docs.text:
Future governance charts may include:

  • quality scoring
  • trust metrics
  • lineage graphs
  • validation coverage

docs.title: AI Visualization Roadmap
docs.text:
Future AI-assisted visualizations may include:

  • anomaly explanations
  • semantic clustering
  • natural language summaries
  • automated chart recommendations

docs.title: Chart Section Control
docs.text:
Charts can be included or excluded.

Example:

aniwa dataset.csv \
  --exclude charts

docs.title: Template Rendering Conditions
docs.text:
Charts render conditionally.

Example:

{% if profile.columns %}

This prevents:

  • empty charts
  • invalid rendering
  • broken layouts

docs.title: PDF Chart Rendering
docs.text:
PDF exports may render charts differently depending on renderer implementation.

Possible future architecture:

Chart.js
→ HTML rendering
→ headless browser
→ PDF snapshot

docs.title: Interactive Chart Vision
docs.text:
Future HTML reports may support:

  • zooming
  • filtering
  • hover analysis
  • cross-highlighting
  • drill-down interactions

docs.title: Enterprise Dashboard Vision
docs.text:
Long-term goals include transforming charts into full dashboards.

Possible future features:

  • live monitoring
  • historical comparisons
  • profiling timelines
  • governance scoring
  • observability integrations

docs.title: Future Plugin System
docs.text:
Future architecture may support custom charts.

Example:

aniwa/plugins/charts/

Allowing:

  • custom visualizations
  • domain-specific dashboards
  • governance plugins
  • ML visualizations

docs.title: Avoid Overcrowding
docs.text: Too many charts reduce readability.

docs.title: Focus on Actionable Insights
docs.text: Charts should answer meaningful questions.

docs.title: Use Appropriate Templates
docs.text:
Examples:

Workflow Template governance enterprise development compact presentations clean engineering dark

docs.title: Assuming Charts Exist in Console Reports
docs.text: Charts currently require HTML or PDF reports.

docs.title: Forgetting to Enable HTML Output
docs.text:
Incorrect:

aniwa dataset.csv --template dark

Correct:

aniwa dataset.csv \
  --report html \
  --template dark

docs.title: Long-Term Vision
docs.text:
Charts are foundational to Aniwa's long-term intelligence platform.

Future systems may evolve into:

  • interactive dashboards

  • enterprise governance visualization
  • AI-assisted data intelligence systems

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
Aniwa charts provide:

  • rapid visual understanding
  • actionable insights
  • governance visibility
  • developer-friendly analytics
  • scalable visualization architecture

They are a core part of making Aniwa:

beautiful, intelligent, and developer-first.

docs.title: CLI Reference
docs.text: This document provides the complete command-line interface reference for Aniwa.

docs.title: Basic Syntax
docs.text:
aniwa [OPTIONS] PATH

Example:

aniwa examples/customers.csv

docs.title: Core Command
docs.text:
Aniwa currently exposes a primary profiling command:

aniwa profile

However, the CLI is designed so users can simply run:

aniwa dataset.csv

docs.title: PATH
docs.text:
Path to dataset file.

Example:

aniwa examples/customers.csv

Supported file types:

  • CSV
  • Excel
  • JSON
  • Parquet

docs.title: --report
docs.text:
Specify report format.

Short form:

-r

Example:

aniwa dataset.csv --report html

Supported values:

  • console
  • json
  • html
  • excel
  • markdown
  • pdf

docs.title: --output
docs.text:
Specify explicit output filename.

Short form:

-o

Example:

aniwa dataset.csv \
  --report html \
  --output profile.html

docs.title: --output-dir
docs.text:
Generate reports into a directory.

Example:

aniwa dataset.csv \
  --report pdf \
  --output-dir reports/

Aniwa automatically:

  • creates directories
  • generates filenames
  • applies extensions

Generated example:

reports/aniwa_report.pdf

docs.title: --mode
docs.text:
Specify profiling mode.

Short form:

-m

Example:

aniwa dataset.csv --mode fast

Supported values:

  • fast
  • deep

docs.title: --include
docs.text:
Include specific report sections.

Short form:

-i

Example:

aniwa dataset.csv \
  --include summary,statistics

docs.title: --exclude
docs.text:
Exclude report sections.

Short form:

-e

Example:

aniwa dataset.csv \
  --exclude charts

docs.title: --template
docs.text:
Specify report template.

Short form:

-t

Example:

aniwa dataset.csv \
  --report html \
  --template dark

Supported templates:

  • default
  • clean
  • compact
  • enterprise
  • dark

docs.title: --config
docs.text:
Specify custom configuration file.

Short form:

-c

Example:

aniwa dataset.csv \
  --config examples/config_sample.yaml

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
Aniwa supports configuration files to simplify reusable profiling workflows.

Configuration files allow users and teams to define default profiling behavior without repeatedly passing CLI arguments.

This improves:

  • reproducibility
  • developer experience
  • automation
  • team workflows
  • CI/CD integration
  • governance consistency

docs.title: Why Configuration Matters
docs.text:
Without configuration files, users must repeatedly type:

aniwa dataset.csv \
  --report html \
  --template dark \
  --mode deep \
  --include summary,statistics,insights

Configuration files solve this problem.

docs.title: Configuration Philosophy
docs.text:
Aniwa configuration is designed to be:

  • lightweight
  • human-readable
  • reproducible
  • extensible
  • automation-friendly

docs.title: Supported Formats
docs.text:
Aniwa currently supports:

Format Extension YAML .yaml, .yml TOML .toml JSON .json

docs.title: Automatic Discovery
docs.text:
Aniwa automatically searches for configuration files in the current working directory.

Search order:

  1. aniwa.yaml
  2. aniwa.yml
  3. aniwa.toml
  4. aniwa.json

docs.title: Example Automatic Usage
docs.text:
If a configuration file exists:

aniwa customers.csv

Aniwa automatically loads configuration values.

docs.title: Custom Configuration Files
docs.text:
Users may explicitly provide configuration files.

Example:

aniwa customers.csv \
  --config configs/production.yaml

docs.title: CLI Override Priority
docs.text:
Configuration priority order:

CLI arguments
→ config file
→ defaults

This means CLI arguments always override configuration values.

docs.title: Example Override
docs.text:
Configuration file:

mode: deep

CLI:

aniwa dataset.csv --mode fast

Final mode:

fast

docs.title: Example YAML Configuration
docs.text:
mode: deep

report:
  format: html
  template: dark
  output_dir: reports/

sections:
  include:
    - summary
    - statistics
    - insights

docs.title: Example TOML Configuration
docs.text:
mode = "deep"

[report]
format = "html"
template = "dark"
output_dir = "reports/"

[sections]
include = ["summary", "statistics", "insights"]

docs.title: Example JSON Configuration
docs.text:
{
  "mode": "deep",
  "report": {
    "format": "html",
    "template": "dark",
    "output_dir": "reports/"
  },
  "sections": {
    "include": [
      "summary",
      "statistics",
      "insights"
    ]
  }
}

docs.title: Current Supported Configuration Fields
docs.text:
Aniwa currently supports:

Field Purpose mode profiling mode report.format report type report.template report template report.output_dir output directory sections.include included sections sections.exclude excluded sections

docs.title: Profiling Mode Configuration
docs.text:
Possible values:

  • fast
  • deep

mode: deep

docs.title: Report Format Configuration
docs.text:

docs.text:

Aniwa automatically generates report filenames.

docs.text:
Example:

sections:
  include:

docs.title: Include vs Exclude
docs.text:
Only one of the following should be used:

  • include
  • exclude

Incorrect:

sections:
  include:
    - summary

  exclude:
    - charts

docs.title: Internal Configuration Flow
docs.text:
Internal loading flow:

CLI
→ load_active_config()
→ get_flattened_config()
→ resolve settings
→ profile execution

docs.title: Configuration Discovery Logic
docs.text:
Discovery function:

discover_config_file()

Responsibilities:

  • search supported filenames
  • detect available config
  • return active config path

docs.title: Internal Config Flattening
docs.text:
Nested config structures are flattened internally.

Example:

report:
  format: html

Becomes:

{
    "report": "html"
}

This simplifies CLI integration.

docs.title: Configuration Validation
docs.text:
Aniwa validates configuration values before profiling begins.

Examples:

  • invalid profiling mode
  • unsupported report format
  • invalid sections

docs.title: Example Invalid Config
docs.text:
mode: ultra

Error:

Invalid profiling mode: ultra

docs.title: Missing Configuration Files
docs.text:
Missing config files do not crash Aniwa.

Aniwa safely falls back to defaults.

docs.title: Invalid File Paths
docs.text:
Invalid explicit config paths raise errors.

Example:

aniwa dataset.csv --config missing.yaml

docs.title: TOML Support
docs.text:
Aniwa uses:

tomllib

Fallback:

tomli

for older Python versions.

docs.title: YAML Support
docs.text:
Aniwa uses:

PyYAML

for YAML parsing.

docs.title: JSON Support
docs.text:
Aniwa uses Python's built-in:

json

module.

docs.title: Metadata
docs.text:
Aniwa generates profiling metadata alongside every dataset profile.

Metadata provides contextual information about:

  • the profiling environment
  • dataset source
  • runtime details
  • report generation settings
  • reproducibility information

Metadata is critical for:

  • governance
  • debugging
  • audits
  • reproducibility
  • CI/CD workflows
  • observability

docs.title: Why Metadata Matters
docs.text:
Without metadata, reports lose context.

Users need to know:

  • when a report was generated
  • which version created it
  • which dataset was profiled
  • what profiling settings were used
  • how long profiling took

Metadata transforms reports into reproducible profiling artifacts.

docs.title: Metadata Architecture
docs.text:
Metadata is represented internally using the ProfileMetadata model.

Location:

aniwa/models/profile.py

docs.title: Metadata Generation Flow
docs.text:
Internal flow:

CLI
→ build_profile_metadata()
→ DatasetProfile.metadata
→ report rendering

docs.title: Metadata Collection
docs.text:
Aniwa collects metadata automatically during profiling.

This requires no additional user configuration.

docs.title: Current Metadata Fields
docs.text:

docs.title: Output Management
docs.text:
Aniwa provides a flexible output management system designed for:

  • reproducibility
  • automation
  • scalability
  • developer workflows
  • CI/CD pipelines
  • governance reporting

Output management controls:

  • report destinations
  • filenames
  • directory handling
  • output generation behavior

docs.title: Why Output Management Matters
docs.text:
Dataset profiling often produces many reports.

Without structured output management:

  • reports become disorganized
  • pipelines become difficult to maintain
  • automation becomes fragile
  • governance workflows become inconsistent

Aniwa solves this through predictable output behavior.

docs.title: Core Output Features
docs.text:
Aniwa currently supports:

  • automatic filenames
  • output directories
  • custom output paths
  • automatic directory creation
  • format-aware extensions
  • output validation

docs.title: Output Architecture
docs.text:
Internal output flow:

CLI
→ resolve_output_path()
→ ensure_output_parent()
→ renderer
→ final artifact

docs.title: Default Output Behavior
docs.text:
When no output path is provided, Aniwa generates default filenames automatically.

Examples:

Format Default Filename html aniwa_report.html json aniwa_report.json markdown aniwa_report.md excel aniwa_report.xlsx pdf aniwa_report.pdf

docs.title: Console Output Exception
docs.text:
Console reports do not generate files.

Example:

aniwa dataset.csv

Output is rendered directly in the terminal.

docs.title: Custom Output Paths
docs.text:
Users can explicitly specify output paths.

Example:

aniwa dataset.csv \
  --report html \
  --output reports/profile.html

docs.title: Output Directory Support
docs.text:
Aniwa supports directory-based report generation.

Example:

aniwa dataset.csv \
  --report pdf \
  --output-dir reports/

Generated output:

reports/aniwa_report.pdf

docs.title: Why Output Directories Matter
docs.text:
Output directories simplify:

  • automation
  • CI/CD
  • batch processing
  • report organization

docs.title: Automatic Directory Creation
docs.text:
Aniwa automatically creates missing directories.

Example:

aniwa dataset.csv \
  --report html \
  --output-dir reports/daily/

Aniwa creates:

reports/
reports/daily/

if they do not already exist.

docs.title: Internal Directory Creation
docs.text:
Internal implementation:

output_path.parent.mkdir(
    parents=True,
    exist_ok=True,
)

Benefits:

  • automation safety
  • reduced setup friction
  • pipeline compatibility

docs.title: Output Validation
docs.text:
Aniwa validates output configuration before report generation.

Examples:

  • conflicting output options
  • invalid formats
  • unsupported templates

docs.title: Mutually Exclusive Options
docs.text:
The following options cannot be used together:

  • --output
  • --output-dir

Incorrect:

aniwa dataset.csv \
  --output report.html \
  --output-dir reports/

Result:

Use either --output or --output-dir, not both.

docs.title: Why Mutual Exclusivity Exists
docs.text:
Allowing both options introduces ambiguity.

Questions include:

  • which path takes precedence?
  • should filenames be overridden?
  • should directories wrap explicit filenames?

Aniwa avoids this complexity through explicit validation.

docs.title: Output Filename Resolution
docs.text:
Internal filename resolution:

resolve_default_name(report)

Example mapping:

{
    ReportFormat.html: "aniwa_report.html"
}

docs.title: Output Extension Resolution
docs.text:
Extensions are automatically matched to report formats.

Examples:

Format Extension html .html json .json markdown .md excel .xlsx pdf .pdf

docs.title: Output Path Resolution Flow
docs.text:
Internal flow:

output specified?
→ yes → use explicit path

output_dir specified?
→ yes → generate filename in directory

otherwise
→ generate default filename

docs.title: Output and Configuration Files
docs.text:
Output settings may be defined in config files.

Example:

report:
  output_dir: reports/

docs.title: CLI Override Priority
docs.text:
Priority order:

CLI > config > defaults

docs.title: Example Override
docs.text:
Config:

report:
  output_dir: reports/

CLI:

aniwa dataset.csv \
  --output final/report.html

Final output:

final/report.html

docs.title: Batch Profiling Workflows
docs.text:
Output management is critical for batch workflows.

Example:

aniwa customers.csv --output-dir reports/
aniwa orders.csv --output-dir reports/
aniwa payments.csv --output-dir reports/

docs.title: CI/CD Workflow Benefits
docs.text:
Output directories improve CI pipelines.

Example:

  --report json \
  --output-dir artifacts/

Benefits:

  • easier uploads

docs.title: Governance Workflow Benefits
docs.text:
Governance workflows often require archival reports.

Example:

  --report pdf \
  --output-dir audits/

docs.title: Profiling Modes
docs.text:
Aniwa supports multiple profiling modes designed for different performance, scalability, and analysis requirements.

Profiling modes determine:

  • profiling depth
  • computational intensity
  • analysis completeness
  • execution speed
  • statistical coverage

docs.title: Available Modes
docs.text:
Aniwa currently supports:

  • fast
  • deep

docs.title: Fast Mode
docs.text:
Fast mode is optimized for speed and lightweight dataset inspection.

Example:

aniwa dataset.csv --mode fast

docs.title: Fast Mode Goals
docs.text:
Fast mode prioritizes:

  • rapid execution
  • lightweight profiling
  • minimal memory overhead
  • fast feedback loops

docs.title: Fast Mode Use Cases
docs.text:
Fast mode is ideal for:

  • CI/CD validation
  • quick inspections
  • debugging pipelines
  • exploratory checks
  • very large datasets
  • development workflows

docs.title: Fast Mode Characteristics
docs.text:
Fast mode generally performs:

  • lightweight schema analysis
  • basic null detection
  • duplicate analysis
  • limited statistics
  • lightweight insights

Fast mode avoids:

  • expensive computations
  • deep statistical analysis
  • heavy cardinality analysis
  • complex heuristics

docs.title: Example Fast Workflow
docs.text:
aniwa production.csv --mode fast

Typical usage:

aniwa production.csv \
  --mode fast \
  --report json

docs.title: Deep Mode
docs.text:
Deep mode performs comprehensive dataset profiling.

Example:

aniwa dataset.csv --mode deep

docs.title: Deep Mode Goals
docs.text:
Deep mode prioritizes:

  • profiling completeness
  • statistical richness
  • intelligent analysis
  • dataset understanding

docs.title: Deep Mode Use Cases
docs.text:
Deep mode is ideal for:

  • audits
  • governance
  • data onboarding
  • quality validation
  • production reviews
  • reporting workflows

docs.title: Deep Mode Characteristics
docs.text:
Deep mode performs:

  • full schema profiling
  • comprehensive quality analysis
  • detailed statistics
  • intelligent heuristics
  • insight generation
  • richer metadata collection

docs.title: Deep Mode Example
docs.text:
aniwa production.csv \
  --mode deep \
  --report pdf

docs.title: Internal Architecture
docs.text:
Profiling mode logic is primarily handled in:

aniwa/core/profiler.py
aniwa/cli.py

docs.title: Runtime Flow
docs.text:
Internally:

CLI
→ resolve mode
→ profiler execution
→ mode-aware profiling
→ report generation

docs.title: Performance Philosophy
docs.text:
Aniwa profiling modes are designed around:

  • scalability
  • flexibility
  • developer experience
  • operational efficiency

docs.title: Fast Mode
docs.text:
Advantages:

  • faster execution
  • lower memory usage
  • ideal for automation

Disadvantages:

  • reduced analytical depth
  • fewer insights
  • less statistical coverage

docs.title: Deep Mode
docs.text:
Advantages:

  • richer analysis
  • more intelligent insights
  • better auditing support

Disadvantages:

  • slower execution
  • more computational work
  • higher memory usage

docs.title: Use Fast Mode For
docs.text:
• CI pipelines
  • pre-validation
  • large datasets
  • exploratory work
  • development loops

Example:

aniwa dataset.csv --mode fast

docs.title: Use Deep Mode For
docs.text:
• production validation
  • governance workflows
  • executive reporting
  • dataset onboarding
  • compliance reviews

Example:

aniwa dataset.csv --mode deep

docs.title: Mode Resolution
docs.text:
Mode resolution follows:

CLI argument
→ config value
→ default value

Default mode:

deep

docs.title: Configuration Example
docs.text:
mode: deep

Override:

aniwa dataset.csv --mode fast

docs.title: Mode-Aware Insights
docs.text:
Future versions of Aniwa may generate:

  • lightweight insights in fast mode
  • advanced semantic insights in deep mode

docs.title: Future Profiling Modes
docs.text: Planned future modes may include:

docs.title: Streaming Mode
docs.text:
For massive datasets:

stream

Potential features:

  • chunked processing
  • incremental profiling
  • low-memory execution

docs.title: Distributed Mode
docs.text:
For distributed compute systems:

distributed

Potential integrations:

  • Spark
  • Ray
  • Dask

docs.title: AI Mode
docs.text:
AI-assisted profiling:

ai

Potential capabilities:

  • semantic understanding
  • anomaly explanations
  • natural language summaries

docs.title: Scalability Strategy
docs.text:
Long-term scalability goals include:

  • lazy execution
  • vectorized analysis
  • chunked computation
  • parallel profiling
  • adaptive heuristics

docs.title: Internal Design Philosophy
docs.text:
Profiling modes are intentionally:

  • extensible
  • modular
  • configurable
  • future-proof

This allows Aniwa to evolve without redesigning the CLI architecture.

docs.title: CI Validation
docs.text: aniwa dataset.csv --mode fast

docs.title: Governance Audit

  --mode deep \

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
Recommended practices:

  • use fast mode in automation
  • use deep mode in audits
  • standardize modes across teams
  • benchmark large datasets
  • profile incrementally when possible

docs.title: Using Deep Mode in CI
docs.text:
Deep mode may increase runtime unnecessarily.

Use:

--mode fast

for CI pipelines.

docs.title: Using Fast Mode for Governance
docs.text:
Fast mode may skip deeper analysis.

Use:

--mode deep

for audits and validation.

docs.title: Report Templates
docs.text:
Aniwa supports multiple report templates designed for different users, workflows, branding styles, and presentation environments.

Templates allow users to customize the visual appearance and layout of generated reports without changing the underlying profiling logic.

docs.title: Why Templates Matter
docs.text:
Different workflows require different reporting styles.

Examples:

  • developers prefer compact reports
  • executives prefer polished dashboards
  • auditors prefer enterprise formatting
  • researchers prefer lightweight layouts

Aniwa templates solve this problem through a modular rendering system.

docs.title: Current Templates
docs.text:
Aniwa currently supports:

  • default
  • clean
  • compact
  • enterprise
  • dark

docs.title: Template Architecture
docs.text:
Templates are implemented as standalone Jinja2 HTML templates.

Location:

aniwa/reports/templates/

Example structure:

aniwa/reports/templates/
├── default.html
├── clean.html
├── compact.html
├── enterprise.html
└── dark.html

docs.text:

CLI
→ render_html_report()

→ Jinja rendering
→ HTML output

docs.title: Template Selection
docs.text:

docs.title: Report Sections
docs.text:
Aniwa reports are modular.

Users can control which sections appear in profiling reports using:

  • --include
  • --exclude

This allows highly focused reports optimized for:

  • debugging
  • governance
  • auditing
  • documentation
  • automation
  • CI pipelines

docs.title: Why Modular Sections Matter
docs.text:
Not every workflow needs every profiling section.

Examples:

Workflow Useful Sections debugging schema, insights governance quality, insights auditing summary, quality, metadata documentation summary, statistics automation JSON schema-only ML validation statistics, insights

Modular reporting keeps reports:

  • smaller
  • faster
  • more readable
  • workflow-specific

docs.title: Available Sections
docs.text:
Aniwa currently supports:

Section Description summary dataset-level overview schema column structure profiling quality nulls, duplicates, uniqueness statistics numeric statistical analysis insights intelligent warnings and observations charts visual profiling charts

docs.title: Section Architecture
docs.text:
Internally, sections are represented using the ReportSection enum.

Example:

class ReportSection(str, Enum):
    summary = "summary"
    schema = "schema"
    quality = "quality"
    statistics = "statistics"
    insights = "insights"
    charts = "charts"

docs.title: Default Behavior
docs.text:
By default, Aniwa includes all sections.

Equivalent behavior:

aniwa dataset.csv

is effectively:

aniwa dataset.csv \
  --include summary,schema,quality,statistics,insights,charts

docs.title: Including Sections
docs.text:
Use --include to explicitly include sections.

Example:

aniwa dataset.csv \
  --include summary,insights

This generates a report containing only:

  • summary
  • insights

docs.title: Excluding Sections
docs.text:
Use --exclude to remove sections.

Example:

aniwa dataset.csv \
  --exclude statistics

This generates a report containing everything except:

  • statistics

docs.title: Important Rule
docs.text:
--include and --exclude cannot be used together.

Incorrect:

aniwa dataset.csv \
  --include summary \
  --exclude charts

This raises an error.

docs.title: Validation System
docs.text:
Aniwa validates section names before profiling begins.

Example invalid command:

aniwa dataset.csv \
  --include metrics

Error:

Invalid report section: metrics

docs.title: Internal Resolution Flow
docs.text:
Section resolution flow:

CLI
→ validate_sections()
→ resolve_sections()
→ profile_dataframe()
→ report rendering

docs.title: Summary Section
docs.text:
The summary section provides dataset-level metrics.

Includes:

  • row count
  • column count
  • dataset size
  • duplicate overview

docs.title: Schema Section
docs.text:
The schema section provides structural profiling.

Includes:

  • column names
  • inferred types
  • type consistency
  • null counts
  • unique counts

docs.title: Quality Section
docs.text:
The quality section analyzes data integrity.

Includes:

  • null analysis
  • duplicate detection
  • uniqueness analysis
  • sparse columns

docs.title: Statistics Section
docs.text:
The statistics section provides numeric profiling.

Includes:

  • minimum
  • maximum
  • mean
  • median
  • standard deviation

Applies only to numeric columns.

docs.title: Insights Section
docs.text:
The insights section provides intelligent observations.

Examples:

  • possible ID columns
  • potential PII detection
  • sparse columns
  • suspicious cardinality
  • duplicate warnings

docs.title: Charts Section
docs.text:
The charts section provides visual analysis.

Includes:

  • null percentage charts
  • unique value charts
  • duplicate charts

Currently available in:

  • HTML reports
  • PDF reports

docs.title: Performance Implications
docs.text: Different sections have different computational costs.

docs.title: Low Cost
docs.text:
Fast sections:

  • summary
  • schema

docs.title: Medium Cost
docs.text:
Moderate sections:

  • quality
  • insights

docs.title: Higher Cost
docs.text:
More expensive sections:

  • statistics
  • charts

Especially on:

  • large datasets
  • wide tables
  • high-cardinality data

docs.title: Fast Mode Interaction
docs.text:
In fast mode, some expensive operations may be reduced.

aniwa dataset.csv --mode fast

Fast mode prioritizes:

  • speed

  • quick inspection

docs.title: Deep Mode Interaction
docs.text:
Deep mode performs complete profiling.

Example:

Deep mode enables:

  • full statistics
  • complete analysis
  • richer insights

Reports dynamically render sections.

{% if profile.columns %}

  • broken charts

docs.text:
